title: Units & Measurement Trivia: Light-Years, the Metric System, and Classic Misconceptions
description: Sixty-four questions on the units we measure the world with — and the famous mistakes people make about them. Six parts: **cosmic distances** (why a *light-year* is a distance and not a time, what a parsec and an astronomical unit really are), the **origin of the metric system** (revolutionary France, the meridian survey, the original metre and kilogram), **SI base units and the 2019 redefinition** (the seven base units, the caesium second, and why the kilogram is now the Planck constant), **temperature scales** (absolute zero, why there is no 'degree Kelvin', and Celsius's upside-down original), **classic unit confusions** (knots, calories, watts vs watt-hours, carats vs karats, and the `$125`-million Mars units disaster), and **everyday conversions and famous numbers**. Many answers are numeric so you practise the figure, not just recognition, and every explanation links to Wikipedia or another source so you can verify it and read further. Several questions deliberately correct a popular but false 'fact'.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: What does a *light-year* measure?

options.text: A distance — how far light travels in one year
An amount of time — one year, as kept by light

questions.explanation: This is the classic misconception the word *year* invites. A light-year is a unit of **distance**: how far light travels through a vacuum in one year — about `9.46` trillion kilometres. Astronomers use it because stellar distances are so vast that kilometres become unwieldy: writing a star as `4.2` light-years away is far clearer than `40,000,000,000,000` km.

questions.title: About how many *trillion kilometres* is one light-year?
questions.description: Enter a number near 9 (in trillions of km).
questions.explanation: Light travels at about `299,792` km every second, and a year is roughly `31.56` million seconds, so a light-year is about `9.46 × 10^12` km — **9.46 trillion** kilometres. The exact IAU figure is `9,460,730,472,580.8` km. That single 'small' unit already spans more than 60,000 times the distance from the Earth to the Sun.

questions.title: About how many *trillion miles* is one light-year?
questions.description: Enter a number near 6 (in trillions of miles).
questions.explanation: Converting the metric figure, one light-year is about `5.88 × 10^12` miles — roughly **5.88 trillion** miles. The number is smaller than the kilometre figure simply because a mile is longer than a kilometre (`1` mile ≈ `1.609` km).

questions.title: When defining a light-year, the International Astronomical Union uses which kind of 'year'?

options.text: A calendar year of exactly `365` days

questions.explanation: To avoid ambiguity, the IAU defines the light-year using the **Julian year** of exactly `365.25` days (`31,557,600` seconds). Multiplying that by the exact speed of light gives a precise, reproducible distance. Using a `365`-day or seasonal year would make the unit wobble slightly, which is unacceptable for a defined standard.

questions.title: Roughly how fast does light travel in a vacuum?
questions.explanation: Light moves at almost exactly `299,792,458` metres per second — close enough to **`300,000` km/s** for mental math. (`343` m/s is the speed of *sound* in air, a different thing entirely.) At that speed light could circle the Earth about 7.5 times in a single second.

questions.title: How long does light from the Sun take to reach the Earth?
questions.explanation: The Sun is about `150` million km away — one astronomical unit — and light covers that in roughly **8 minutes and 20 seconds** (about `499` seconds). So you always see the Sun as it looked about eight minutes ago; if it vanished, we would not know for those eight minutes.

questions.title: About how far is one *light-second*?
questions.explanation: A light-second is the distance light travels in one second: about **`300,000` km** (`299,792` km, to be precise). The Moon is about `1.28` light-seconds away, which is why a two-way radio chat with astronauts on the Moon had a noticeable lag. The `9.46`-trillion-km figure is a light-*year*.

questions.title: In *Star Wars*, Han Solo boasts of making the Kessel Run 'in less than twelve parsecs.' What does a parsec actually measure?
questions.explanation: Like the light-year, a **parsec is a unit of distance** (about `3.26` light-years), so bragging about a *time* in parsecs is the famous flub. Fans have since rationalised the line as Solo finding a shorter *route*, but taken literally it confuses distance with speed.

questions.title: About how many *light-years* is one parsec?
questions.explanation: One parsec ≈ **`3.26`** light-years (about `30.9` trillion km). Astronomers often prefer parsecs because the unit falls straight out of the parallax method used to measure stellar distances — see the next question.

questions.title: Where does the *parsec* get its name and definition?

options.text: The distance at which `1` astronomical unit subtends a parallax angle of one arcsecond
The distance light travels while a pulsar ticks once
One percent of the distance to the nearest star
The radius of the Solar System

questions.explanation: *Parsec* is a contraction of '**par**allax of one arc**sec**ond.' It is the distance at which the radius of Earth's orbit (`1` AU) appears one arcsecond (`1/3600` of a degree) across. Because a star's measured parallax angle directly gives its distance in parsecs, the unit is woven into how the distances are actually obtained.

questions.title: What is an *astronomical unit (AU)*?

options.text: Roughly the average distance from the Earth to the Sun
The distance from the Sun to the edge of the Solar System
The distance light travels in one day

questions.explanation: The astronomical unit is based on the **average Earth–Sun distance** and is now fixed at exactly `149,597,870,700` metres (about `150` million km). It is the natural yardstick for distances *inside* the Solar System, where light-years would be absurdly small fractions.

questions.title: About how many *million kilometres* is one astronomical unit?
questions.description: Enter a number near 150 (in millions of km).
questions.explanation: One AU is about **`149.6` million km** (exactly `149,597,870.7` km). Handy anchors that follow from it: light crosses `1` AU in about 8 minutes, and `1` parsec is the distance at which that `1` AU baseline spans a single arcsecond.

questions.title: Which is the nearest star to the Sun?
questions.explanation: **Proxima Centauri**, a faint red dwarf in the Alpha Centauri system, lies about **`4.24` light-years** (`1.30` parsecs) from the Sun — roughly `40` trillion km. Even at this, the closest possible interstellar distance, light still takes over four years to arrive, which is why crewed travel to other stars is so daunting.

questions.title: Why is it *wrong* to say 'the journey took ten light-years'?

options.text: Because a light-year measures distance, so it answers 'how far', not 'how long'
Because ten light-years is too short for any journey
Because light-years only apply to light, not spacecraft
Because the correct unit would be light-decades

questions.explanation: A duration must be given in time units (seconds, years), but a **light-year is a distance**. 'The journey took ten light-years' mixes the two, like saying 'the trip took ten kilometres.' You *can* say a destination is ten light-years *away* — that is a distance — or that light from it takes ten years to arrive.

questions.title: In which country did the modern metric system originate?
questions.explanation: The metric system was created in **France** in the 1790s, during the French Revolution. Reformers wanted to sweep away a chaos of local units that varied from town to town and replace them with one rational, decimal system 'for all people, for all time.'

questions.title: How was the metre originally defined in the 1790s?

options.text: As one ten-millionth of the distance from the North Pole to the Equator, along the meridian through Paris
As the length of the king's forearm
As one-millionth of the Earth's radius
As the height of an average adult

questions.explanation: The revolutionaries tied the metre to the planet itself: **one ten-millionth of the quarter-meridian** running from the North Pole to the Equator through Paris. The idea was a unit grounded in nature rather than in any monarch's body, so that it could in principle be re-derived anywhere.

questions.title: To fix the metre, surveyors Delambre and Méchain measured the meridian arc between which two cities?
questions.explanation: From 1792 to 1799, Jean-Baptiste Delambre and Pierre Méchain triangulated the meridian between **Dunkirk** (on the English Channel) and **Barcelona** (on the Mediterranean), both near sea level and on the Paris meridian. From that arc they extrapolated the pole-to-equator distance and so the length of the metre — a heroic, seven-year survey carried out through revolution and war.

questions.title: The metric system scales its units using which base?

options.text: `10` — units relate by powers of ten

questions.explanation: Metric units are **decimal**: a kilometre is `1,000` metres, a centimetre is `1/100` of a metre, and conversions are just shifts of the decimal point. Compare the older units it replaced — `12` inches to a foot, `16` ounces to a pound, `60` minutes to an hour — where every conversion needs a different factor.

questions.title: How was the original kilogram defined?

options.text: As the mass of one litre of water, near its densest
As the mass of a fixed lump of gold
As the mass of one cubic metre of air
As the weight of a standard loaf of bread

questions.explanation: The kilogram was originally the mass of **one litre of water** (a cube `10` cm on a side) at the temperature of its maximum density, near `4 °C`. This tied mass neatly to length through water — though water's mass is fussy to reproduce exactly, which is why a metal prototype soon took over.

questions.title: The word *metre* comes from which root?
questions.explanation: *Metre* descends, via French *mètre*, from the Greek **_metron_ ('a measure')** — fittingly, the unit's name simply means 'measure.' The same root gives us *thermometer*, *barometer*, and *symmetry*.

questions.title: In the modern system of units, what does the abbreviation *SI* stand for?

options.text: Système International d'Unités (the International System of Units)

questions.explanation: *SI* abbreviates the French **_Système International d'Unités_** — the International System of Units, the modern, globally agreed form of the metric system. The abbreviation is the same in every language precisely because it is taken from the French name.

questions.title: In which year was the modern system formally named the 'Système International d'Unités' (SI)?
questions.explanation: The name *Système International d'Unités* and the abbreviation *SI* were adopted in **1960** by the 11th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM). It built on the older metric system, organising everything around a small set of base units.

questions.title: The US customary units (inches, pounds, gallons) derive mainly from which source?

options.text: English / British Imperial units brought by colonists
Units invented from scratch in the US in 1776

questions.explanation: US customary units descend from **English units** carried across by colonists, the same family that later became the British Imperial system. The two have since drifted apart in places — most famously the gallon — which is why US and UK 'gallons' and 'tons' are not the same size.

questions.title: How many *base units* does the SI define?
questions.explanation: There are **seven** SI base units: the metre (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity). Every other SI unit — the newton, joule, watt, volt, and so on — is *derived* by combining these seven.

questions.title: Which of the following are *SI base units*?
questions.explanation: The seven base units are the metre, kilogram, second, **ampere**, **kelvin**, **mole**, and **candela**. The litre, newton, and watt are *not* base units: the litre is a non-SI unit of volume (`1 L = 1 dm^3`), while the newton (`kg·m/s^2`) and watt (`J/s`) are *derived* from base units.

questions.title: What is the SI base unit of *mass*?
questions.explanation: The base unit of mass is the **kilogram**, not the gram — the only base unit whose very name already carries a prefix ('kilo-'). The newton is a unit of *force* (`mass × acceleration`), and the pound belongs to the customary system, not SI.

questions.title: Which SI base unit is unusual because its name already contains a metric *prefix*?
questions.explanation: The **kilogram** is the oddity: 'kilo-' normally means `× 1,000`, yet here it is baked into the *base* unit itself. A consequence is that multiples of mass are built on the *gram* (milligram, gram, tonne) even though the kilogram is the official base unit — a quirk left over from history.

questions.title: Since 1983, how is the *metre* officially defined?

options.text: As the distance light travels in a vacuum in `1/299,792,458` of a second
As the length of a platinum bar kept in Paris
As one ten-millionth of the pole-to-equator distance
As `1,650,763.73` wavelengths of orange krypton-86 light

questions.explanation: Since 1983 the metre is defined from the **speed of light**: the distance light travels in a vacuum in `1/299,792,458` of a second. This makes the speed of light *exactly* `299,792,458` m/s by definition. The other three options are all genuine *former* definitions — the meridian (1790s), the platinum bar (1889), and the krypton-86 wavelength (1960) — now retired.

questions.title: How is the SI *second* defined today?

options.text: By a fixed number of microwave oscillations of the caesium-133 atom
As `1/86,400` of a mean solar day
By a pendulum exactly one metre long
By the vibration of a standard quartz crystal

questions.explanation: Since 1967 the second has been defined by the **caesium-133 atom**: the duration of `9,192,631,770` cycles of the radiation between two of its energy levels — the basis of atomic clocks. The old 'fraction of a day' definition was abandoned because the Earth's rotation is slightly irregular and gradually slowing.

questions.title: Exactly how many oscillations of the caesium-133 atom define one second?
questions.explanation: One second is exactly **`9,192,631,770`** periods of the caesium-133 hyperfine transition. The oddly specific number was chosen in 1967 to match the older astronomical second as closely as possible, so clocks would not jump when the definition changed. (`299,792,458` is the *speed of light* in m/s — a different famous constant.)

questions.title: In the 2019 overhaul of the SI, the base units were redefined in terms of what?

options.text: Fixed numerical values of fundamental physical constants
The average of national prototype standards
Fresh measurements of the Earth's size

questions.explanation: On 20 May 2019 the SI was rebuilt on **fundamental constants** of nature — fixing exact values for the Planck constant, the elementary charge, the Boltzmann constant, and the Avogadro constant, among others. The goal is permanence: a constant of physics cannot be lost, damaged, or drift like a manufactured object.

questions.title: Since 2019, the kilogram is defined using which fundamental constant?
questions.explanation: The kilogram is now fixed via the **Planck constant** (`h = 6.62607015 × 10^-34` joule-seconds), realised in the lab with a Kibble balance that pits mechanical against electrical power. The speed of light defines the *metre*, and the Avogadro constant defines the *mole* — so this question is really about matching each constant to its unit.

questions.title: The retired International Prototype of the Kilogram — 'Le Grand K' — was made of what?
questions.explanation: Made in the 1880s, the prototype was a cylinder of **`90%` platinum and `10%` iridium** — a dense, hard, chemically stable alloy. Yet even it was not perfectly constant: comparisons with its official copies suggested its mass was drifting by tens of micrograms, which is exactly why the kilogram was redefined in 2019.

questions.title: In which year was the physical kilogram prototype retired in favour of a constant-based definition?
questions.explanation: The International Prototype of the Kilogram was retired on **20 May 2019**, when the Planck-constant definition took effect. It was the last SI base unit still tied to a manufactured object, so its retirement completed the shift to a system built entirely on constants of nature.

questions.title: The *mole* is the SI base unit for which quantity?

options.text: Amount of substance (a count of particles)

questions.explanation: A mole measures **amount of substance** — essentially a fixed *count* of particles (atoms, molecules, ions). It lets chemists bridge the atomic scale and the laboratory bench: one mole of any substance contains the same number of particles, the Avogadro number.

questions.title: Avogadro's number is about how many particles per mole, written as a coefficient times `10^23`?
questions.description: Enter the coefficient — a value near 6.
questions.explanation: One mole contains about **`6.02 × 10^23`** particles. Since 2019 the figure is fixed *exactly* at `6.02214076 × 10^23`, which is now the definition of the mole rather than a measured quantity. The number is staggering: a mole of sand grains would bury entire continents.

questions.title: What is absolute zero, in degrees Celsius?
questions.description: Enter a value near −273 (include the minus sign).
questions.explanation: Absolute zero — `0` kelvin, the coldest temperature physically possible — is **`−273.15 °C`**. It is the point at which a system has the minimum possible thermal energy. The Kelvin scale simply starts here, which is what makes it 'absolute,' so `0 K = −273.15 °C` and `0 °C = 273.15 K`.

questions.title: Which is the *correct* way to write a temperature of three hundred on the Kelvin scale?

options.text: `300 K` — no degree symbol, no word 'degrees'

questions.explanation: It is just **`300 K`** — 'three hundred kelvin.' Since 1967 the unit has taken **no degree symbol** and is *not* read as 'degrees Kelvin'; that common phrasing is the misconception. The kelvin is an absolute unit, not measured from an arbitrary zero like Celsius or Fahrenheit, so the 'degree' was dropped.

questions.title: At what temperature do the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales read the *same* number?
questions.description: Enter the value (include the minus sign).
questions.explanation: The scales cross at **`−40°`**: `−40 °C = −40 °F`. You can prove it by setting `C = (C × 9/5) + 32` and solving, which gives `C = −40`. It is the one temperature where you never have to say which scale you mean.

questions.title: At standard sea-level pressure, water boils at how many degrees *Fahrenheit*?
questions.explanation: Water boils at **`212 °F`** (`100 °C`) and freezes at `32 °F` (`0 °C`) at standard pressure — exactly `180` Fahrenheit degrees between the two fixed points, versus `100` Celsius degrees, which is why a Fahrenheit degree is smaller (`5/9` of a Celsius degree).

questions.title: Normal human body temperature is conventionally quoted as about how many degrees *Fahrenheit*?
questions.explanation: The familiar figure is **`98.6 °F`** — which is simply `37.0 °C` converted. That suspiciously precise decimal is an artefact of conversion: the original `37 °C` is a round number, and modern studies suggest average body temperature is actually a little *below* `37 °C`.

questions.title: When Anders Celsius first proposed his scale in 1742, how was it arranged?

options.text: Reversed — `0` marked boiling water and `100` marked melting ice
Exactly as we use it today

questions.explanation: Celsius's original 1742 scale was **upside-down** by modern eyes: `0` was the *boiling* point of water and `100` the *melting* point of ice. It was flipped to today's orientation around 1744 (commonly credited to Carl Linnaeus, with Jean-Pierre Christin proposing the same independently in 1743).

questions.title: What did Daniel Fahrenheit originally use to set `0 °F`?

options.text: The temperature of a freezing mixture of ice, water, and salt
The freezing point of pure water
The average winter temperature in his home town

questions.explanation: Fahrenheit set his zero at the stable low temperature of a **brine** — a frigorific mix of ice, water, and ammonium-chloride salt — the coldest he could reliably reproduce in 1724. He then placed the freezing point of pure water at `32` and (originally) body temperature near `96`. Pure water's freezing point is `32 °F`, not `0 °F`, which surprises many people.

questions.title: A temperature *change* of one kelvin equals a change of how much on the Celsius scale?

options.text: Exactly `1 °C` — the kelvin and the Celsius degree are the same size

questions.explanation: The Kelvin and Celsius scales share the same *degree size*: a rise of `1 K` is a rise of exactly **`1 °C`**. They differ only in where zero sits (`0 K = −273.15 °C`). So you convert by adding or subtracting `273.15`, never by multiplying. The `1.8` factor belongs to Fahrenheit, whose degree is smaller.

questions.title: In navigation, what does a *knot* measure?

options.text: Speed — one nautical mile per hour

questions.explanation: A knot is a unit of **speed**: one nautical mile per hour. Because the 'per hour' is already inside the definition, the phrase 'knots per hour' is wrong — it would mean *acceleration*. The name comes from the old log line, a rope with evenly spaced knots paid out behind a ship to clock its speed.

questions.title: How does a *nautical mile* compare with an ordinary (statute) land mile?

options.text: It is longer — about `1,852 m` versus about `1,609 m`
They are exactly the same length

questions.explanation: A nautical mile (`1,852 m`) is about `15%` **longer** than a statute mile (`1,609 m`). They are different units for different worlds: the statute mile is a land measure of Roman ancestry, while the nautical mile is tied to the geometry of the globe — see the next question.

questions.title: What is a nautical mile historically based on?

options.text: One minute of arc of latitude along a meridian
One degree of longitude at the equator
The distance a ship sails in one hour

questions.explanation: A nautical mile was defined as **one minute of latitude** — `1/60` of a degree — along a meridian. That makes it wonderfully practical at sea: one minute of latitude on a chart simply *is* one nautical mile, so distance can be stepped straight off the latitude scale. It is now fixed at exactly `1,852 m`.

questions.title: The 'Calorie' listed on food nutrition labels is equal to what?
questions.explanation: The food **Calorie** (often capitalised) is actually a **kilocalorie**: `1,000` of the 'small' calories used in physics, or about `4,184` joules. So a `200`-Calorie snack really holds `200,000` small calories. The two units sharing a name causes endless confusion; outside nutrition, scientists mostly use joules.

questions.title: Which statement about the *watt* and the *watt-hour* is correct?

options.text: The watt measures power (a rate); the watt-hour and kilowatt-hour measure energy
They are two names for the same thing
The watt measures energy; the watt-hour measures power
A kilowatt-hour measures power used over a distance

questions.explanation: A **watt is power** — energy *per second* (`1 W = 1 J/s`). A **watt-hour is energy** — power multiplied by time. A `1,000 W` heater running for one hour uses `1` kilowatt-hour, the unit on your electricity bill. This is why 'kilowatts per hour' is a red flag: power is already a per-time rate, so the phrase double-counts time.

questions.title: About how many *watts* is one (mechanical) horsepower?
questions.explanation: One mechanical (imperial) horsepower is about **`746` watts** (`745.7 W`, defined as `550` foot-pounds-force per second). Watch out: the *metric* horsepower (PS) is slightly different at about `735.5 W`, so engine figures can disagree depending on which 'horsepower' is meant. James Watt coined the unit to market his steam engines against draft horses.

questions.title: Which statement about the word *ton* is correct?

options.text: It is ambiguous: a US short ton is `2,000 lb`, a UK long ton `2,240 lb`, and a metric tonne `1,000 kg`
It always means exactly `1,000 kg` everywhere
It always means exactly `2,000` pounds everywhere
It is an official SI unit

questions.explanation: 'Ton' is genuinely **ambiguous**. The US *short ton* is `2,000 lb` (≈ `907 kg`), the British *long ton* is `2,240 lb` (≈ `1,016 kg`), and the metric *tonne* is `1,000 kg`. They are close but not equal, so the spelling 'tonne' (or 'metric ton') is used when the `1,000 kg` meaning matters.

questions.title: What is the difference between a *carat* and a *karat*?

options.text: A carat is a mass for gemstones (`200 mg`); a karat is the purity of gold (`24`-karat is pure)
They are two spellings of one identical unit
Both measure the purity of gold

questions.explanation: A **carat** (with a *c*) is a unit of *mass* for gemstones, fixed at exactly `200` milligrams. A **karat** (with a *k*, in US usage) measures the *purity* of gold in `24ths`: `24`-karat is pure gold, `18`-karat is `18/24 = 75%` gold. Same-sounding words, completely different quantities — and in British English both are spelled 'carat', which makes it worse.

questions.title: How does an earthquake magnitude scale (Richter / moment magnitude) work?

options.text: Logarithmically — each whole number is about `10×` the ground-shaking amplitude
Linearly — a magnitude `6` is twice a magnitude `3`
It is capped at a maximum of `10`
It directly counts the number of deaths

questions.explanation: Earthquake magnitude is **logarithmic**: each whole step up multiplies the measured wave amplitude by about `10`, and the *energy* released by about `32×`. So a magnitude `7` is not 'a bit worse' than a `5` — it shakes `100×` as hard and releases roughly `1,000×` the energy. The scale has no fixed upper limit (the modern version is the moment magnitude scale).

questions.title: What kind of scale is the *decibel*?

options.text: A logarithmic ratio between two power or intensity levels
A linear, absolute unit of loudness
A count of sound waves per second

questions.explanation: The decibel is **logarithmic** and **relative**: it expresses a *ratio* of powers. Adding `10 dB` means `10×` the power; adding `3 dB` roughly *doubles* it. That is why a `90 dB` sound carries vastly more power than a `60 dB` one, not merely `50%` more. The 'bel' honours Alexander Graham Bell; a decibel is a tenth of a bel.

questions.title: In 1999, NASA lost the `$125`-million Mars Climate Orbiter. What went wrong?

options.text: A units mix-up — one team used pound-force seconds while the other expected newton-seconds
It simply ran out of fuel
It was struck by a meteoroid
A typo sent it to the wrong planet

questions.explanation: The spacecraft was doomed by a **units error**: Lockheed Martin's software reported thruster impulse in **pound-force seconds** (US customary), while NASA's navigation software assumed **newton-seconds** (metric). The forces were off by a factor of about `4.45`, so the probe dipped far too low — near `57 km` instead of the planned `~226 km` — and was destroyed in the Martian atmosphere. It is the textbook cautionary tale for always stating your units.

questions.title: How many *feet* are there in one (statute) mile?
questions.explanation: There are **`5,280`** feet in a mile (equivalently `1,760` yards). The awkward number is a historical compromise: the mile came from the Roman *mille passus* ('a thousand paces'), later reconciled with the furlong (`660` feet) used in English field measurement — `8` furlongs make a mile.

questions.title: How many *metres* are there in one kilometre?
questions.explanation: Exactly **`1,000`** — the prefix 'kilo-' means `× 1,000`. This is the whole appeal of the metric system: every step is a clean power of ten, so converting is just moving the decimal point, with none of the `5,280`-feet-per-mile arithmetic.

questions.title: How many *inches* are there in one foot?
questions.explanation: There are **`12`** inches in a foot. Twelve is a 'duodecimal' choice that long predates the metric system, prized because it divides evenly into halves, thirds, quarters, and sixths — handy before calculators, but a nuisance when chaining conversions (`12` inches/foot, then `3` feet/yard, then `1,760` yards/mile).

questions.title: In the British system, how many *pounds* are there in one stone?
questions.explanation: One stone is **`14`** pounds (about `6.35 kg`). The stone is still widely used for body weight in the UK and Ireland — someone might say they weigh '`11` stone `4`' — even though it is rarely used elsewhere and is not part of US customary measure.

questions.title: The metric prefix *giga-* means a factor of what?
questions.explanation: *Giga-* means **`10^9`**, a billion — so a gigabyte is about a billion bytes and a gigawatt is a billion watts. Its neighbours go *mega-* (`10^6`), giga- (`10^9`), *tera-* (`10^12`), each a thousand times the last. The word comes from the Greek *gigas*, 'giant.'

questions.title: Which of these metric-prefix values are *correct*?
questions.explanation: Correct: kilo- (`10^3`), mega- (`10^6`), milli- (`10^-3`), micro- (`10^-6`). The two traps: **centi-** is `10^-2` (a hundredth, as in centimetre), *not* `10^-3`; and **nano-** is `10^-9` (a billionth), *not* `10^-6` — that value belongs to micro-.

questions.title: How does a US gallon compare with an imperial (UK) gallon?

options.text: The imperial gallon is larger — about `4.55 L` versus the US gallon's `3.79 L`

questions.explanation: They are *not* the same: the **imperial gallon** is about `4.546` litres, roughly `20%` larger than the **US gallon** of about `3.785` litres. So a 'gallon' of fuel economy or a recipe's 'gallon' means different amounts on each side of the Atlantic — a frequent source of conversion errors.

questions.title: Which trio of countries is most often cited as *not* having officially adopted the metric system?

options.text: The United States, Liberia, and Myanmar (Burma)
The United States, Canada, and Australia
The United Kingdom, the United States, and Japan

questions.explanation: The popular trivia answer is the **United States, Liberia, and Myanmar**. But treat it as an oversimplification: *every* country uses metric units to some degree (science, medicine, and trade are metric almost everywhere), and these three simply have not fully *officially* adopted SI for everyday use — and both Liberia and Myanmar have moved toward metrication. So the statement is a useful headline, not a hard fact.

questions.title: One *ångström* equals what length?
questions.explanation: An ångström is **`10^-10` m**, or `0.1` nanometre — a convenient size for the atomic world, since typical atomic radii and chemical bond lengths are a few ångströms. It is named after the Swedish physicist Anders Jonas Ångström. (`10^-15` m is the femtometre, the scale of atomic *nuclei*.)
